posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
William Kelley joins Wine Berserkers again for an online Zoom Q&A session, fresh after En Primeur, on Friday May 6th at 11 am Pacific, 2pm Eastern (8pm France time)

I’ll have a waiting room setup and will have video and audio on, though I will turn everyone’s audio OFF (since, inevitably, people forget and start yelling at spouses or young kids or dogs make noise in the background) until the Q&A session starts, which is pretty much right away, unless William has some wise words for us to start with as our long intro. We’ll use the ‘raise hand’ and chat functions to manage the questions, and hopefully since it will be 8pm William’s time he will be two sheets in the wind by then, so he can speak with loose lips.

Zoom Video

Join our Cloud HD Video Meeting

Zoom is the leader in modern enterprise video communications, with an easy, reliable cloud platform for video and audio conferencing, chat, and webinars across mobile, desktop, and room systems. Zoom Rooms is the original software-based conference...

Date: Friday May 6, 2022
Time: 11am Pacific/2pm Eastern

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Thank you William and Todd, that was highly engaging and interesting. I learned a bit more about wine. And there are fish in the river in Champagne!

On the first topic - re-setting the 100 point scale and making wine criticism more meaningful. I very much salute the project and your commitment to it. The value and meaning of your ratings are part of why I find you an engaging and exciting reviewer. I hope you sway a few more to your point of view, but I (perhaps cynically) doubt that the ‘system’ is entirely fixable. The financials are too strong and the points are too tied up in people’s livelihoods. But for those of us who care, we are better for your efforts.

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked: Thank you William and Todd. That was great.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Enjoyed what I could be on for. Stupid work meeting overlapped the Zoom.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thank you William and Todd, that was highly engaging and interesting. I learned a bit more about wine. And there are fish in the river in Champagne!

On the first topic - re-setting the 100 point scale and making wine criticism more meaningful. I very much salute the project and your commitment to it. The value and meaning of your ratings are part of why I find you an engaging and exciting reviewer. I hope you sway a few more to your point of view, but I (perhaps cynically) doubt that the ‘system’ is entirely fixable. The financials are too strong and the points are too tied up in people’s livelihoods. But for those of us who care, we are better for your efforts.

I actually feel quite confident about it! Unlike Mark Twain (to slightly adapt the quotation), I have faith in the intelligence of the consumer!

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Which wine was 47-57 pounds ex-domaine?

I believe that was Coche Meursault. But who needs to buy it from the Domaine when it is available for such modest markups here in California? Money - they literally print the stuff. Cool that we can turn just a few scraps of paper into wine!

posts.name: Bri@n Kelly
posts.cooked:
Furthermore, William has promised to post the ‘under the radar’ Burgundy producers he rattled off in record time (the dude speaks quite quickly, I must say) herein.

While I’m not William, and he’ll correct me if I’m wrong, he listed Didier Fornerol, Cyril Audoin (Domaine Charles Audoin) and Boris Champy.

Shhhh, quiet on Audoin. It’s already started to get pricey and hard to find. My wife and I sat with Cyril at IPNC in 2016 where he shared one of his Marsannay blancs (I don’t remember which one) with us and completely opened our previously shut eyes to white burgundy.

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Furthermore, William has promised to post the ‘under the radar’ Burgundy producers he rattled off in record time (the dude speaks quite quickly, I must say) herein.

While I’m not William, and he’ll correct me if I’m wrong, he listed Didier Fornerol, Cyril Audoin (Domaine Charles Audoin) and Boris Champy.

Shhhh, quiet on Audoin. It’s already started to get pricey and hard to find. My wife and I sat with Cyril at IPNC in 2016 where he shared one of his Marsannay blancs (I don’t remember which one) with us and completely opened our previously shut eyes to white burgundy.

Audoin is the only one of the three Greg listed (based on what William said) that I actually own bottles of. (No dis to William but while I can pronounce some written French in my way, hearing it spoken it is almost impossible for me to take notes.)

My search for Burgundy I can afford led me to Audoin and to buy or keep an eye out for (in no particular order):
Elodie Roy
Jean Javillier
Michel Rebourgeon (in the William Whitehead era)
Pierre Guillemot
Pierre Brisset
Guillemot-Michel (whites) (active here)

I own but have not necessarily tasted some of them.

After I mentioned Pierre Guillemot to William, I was hoping he’d mention some of these and others and was glad to hear him mention Audoin (after Greg interpreted it here).

posts.name: C. Keller
posts.cooked:
Really enjoyed this.
Especially the opening monologue about how Bordeaux estates are corporations; financials that take into account KPI’s (scores) and a corporate hierarchy for the personnel where the owner and wine maker are usually different people.

Whereas in burgundy, well, the hype drives the price and the owner and winemaker are usually the same person, although not everywhere.

Btw, what was in your glass?

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked: I’ve been picking up some of those WK recommendations as well as a few others. If you read his reviews in RPWA there are quite a lot of producers that William recommends beyond the loftiest of names. Especially if you go beyond the Côte d’Or. Far too many to buy all of them though (sadly for my credit card and wine storage) I have been trying!

posts.name: Justin S ! n g h
posts.cooked:
I like two of Audoin’s 2017 reds that I’ve tried: Favieres and Longeroies. Favieres is really full-bodied, like whole milk, bursting with dense fruit, but you can sense the substantial structure underneath. Longeroies is a bit lighter in body, brighter fruit, perhaps more elegant, but still very concentrated in flavor in this vintage and showing some strong tannins on day 2. Favieres probably will age longer because there is just more material there, amped up to 11. If I had to pick one, Longeroies is more my style for drinking today, but Favieres probably has the higher “score”.

I’m sure WK’s tasting notes on these two are much better, but my 2c for anyone taking a look.

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
I like two of Audoin’s 2017 reds that I’ve tried: Favieres and Longeroies. Favieres is really full-bodied, like whole milk, bursting with dense fruit, but you can sense the substantial structure underneath. Longeroies is a bit lighter in body, brighter fruit, perhaps more elegant, but still very concentrated in flavor in this vintage and showing some strong tannins on day 2. Favieres probably will age longer because there is just more material there, amped up to 11. If I had to pick one, Longeroies is more my style for drinking today, but Favieres probably has the higher “score”.

I’m sure WK’s tasting notes on these two are much better, but my 2c for anyone taking a look.

The one I got was the Champ Salomon because, as you say about the Longeroies, it sounded less dense than the Favières but was scored as highly by William, at least in 2019. Based on your take, I’d probably enjoy the Longeroies as well. I know Jasper isn’t quite as keen on these as William is but am not sure why. I plan to again get the Champ Salomon for 2020 when it’s available and might just try the Longeroies as well.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Work meetings got in the way so I couldn’t join. But one question I had/have is around the still wines of Champagne. Is this a movement with real growth/energy behind it like the grower movement of the last few decades, or is it more of a passing fad?

It’s still tiny, but there are a lot of new entrants. Much to my regret, there’s a big tasting of côteaux organized by the more serious producers happening Tuesday that I can’t attend.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Really enjoyed this.
Especially the opening monologue about how Bordeaux estates are corporations; financials that take into account KPI’s (scores) and a corporate hierarchy for the personnel where the owner and wine maker are usually different people.

Whereas in burgundy, well, the hype drives the price and the owner and winemaker are usually the same person, although not everywhere.

Btw, what was in your glass?

2010 La Fleur-Pétrus! Young wine for a change…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I like two of Audoin’s 2017 reds that I’ve tried: Favieres and Longeroies. Favieres is really full-bodied, like whole milk, bursting with dense fruit, but you can sense the substantial structure underneath. Longeroies is a bit lighter in body, brighter fruit, perhaps more elegant, but still very concentrated in flavor in this vintage and showing some strong tannins on day 2. Favieres probably will age longer because there is just more material there, amped up to 11. If I had to pick one, Longeroies is more my style for drinking today, but Favieres probably has the higher “score”.

I’m sure WK’s tasting notes on these two are much better, but my 2c for anyone taking a look.

They can age and improve for a long time, too!

Because of comparatively modest pricing, I suspect a lot get drunk up on the young side, yet patience really does bear dividends.

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked: Is there a resolution beyond 360p? I would like to enjoy Sir Kelley in high definition

Thanks for doing this, William!

one thing that you mentioned I really wanted to get your thoughts on though: and that is that I’ve always found Bordeaux far harder to approach in youth than Piedmont wines. I thought it was fascinating that you enjoy and are carving this niche in Bordeaux reviewing, but talked about the massive structure in Nebbiolo as a reason for not getting as into that region. I was just wondering if you could comment on this a little bit? what do you do to make Bordeaux more approachable when tasting? because I’ll admit approachability is a big part of the reason my cellar is something like 1/3 Italian wines but I only own 3 bottles of Bordeaux!
